query: does radiotherapy for cervical cancer increase risk of colon cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Radiation Therapy Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Long-term toxicity is a concern with any patient receiving radiation therapy. Bowel, rectal, and urinary complications are the most frequent. There does not appear to be a difference in the frequency or severity of complications with respect to age. The greatest risk of late sequelae typically occurs within 3 years of treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Secondary malignancy
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation-induced cancers tend to appear several decades after treatment. The overall risk of secondary malignancy is increased with pelvic radiotherapy compared to those treated with surgery alone. Women younger than 50 years had a 40-year cumulative risk of 22% versus 16% for those older than 50 years. [74] Secondary cancers tend to be confined to the rectum, bladder, lungs, and genitals. Other retrospective studies have shown an increase in leukemia risk, which peaks around 5 to 10 years after treatment. [75] Limiting normal organ doses may help to reduce the risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- BT dose and dose constraints
retrieved_docs.content: Doses to OAR must also be carefully documented. Again, it is the cumulative dose of EQD2 that is critical. The dosimetric parameter D2 cc, which represents the highest dose received by 2 ccs of tissue, is commonly used for evaluating a BT plan. The American Brachytherapy Society (ABS) guidelines allow for the D2 cc of the bladder to receive 90 Gy or less EQD2, whereas the D2 cc of the rectum and sigmoid should be 75 Gy or less EQD2. However, recent data suggest that late rectal morbidity may be substantially lower even using a D2 cc of 65 Gy or less. [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Enteritis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Ongoing studies have shown an increased risk of malignancy as a late consequence of pelvic radiation.

retrieved_docs.title: Colitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation colitis: This can occur secondary to pelvic radiotherapy for gynecological, urological and rectal cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Proctitis -- Treatment / Management -- Primary Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: While adherence to established dose constraints is essential, how radiotherapy is delivered can also influence the risk of late rectal toxicity. When comparing 3D conformal treatment to IMRT when treating the pelvis, the incidence of late rectal toxicity appears to be lower with IMRT in the treatment of genitourinary and gynecologic malignancies. Late rectal toxicity in patients treated with 3D radiation for prostate cancer was 13% versus 5% in those treated with IMRT. [17] Postoperative endometrial and cervical cancer patients treated with IMRT also demonstrate lower rates of late gastrointestinal (GI) toxicity compared to 3D conformal (42% vs 21%). [18] [19] Despite the claims that proton therapy will lead to even lower rates of toxicity, the data suggest that proton therapy, at best, has no effect and, at worst, may have higher rates of GI toxicity compared to IMRT for patients with prostate cancer. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Proctitis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation proctitis refers to injury or damage to the rectum secondary to radiation therapy. It is postulated that almost half of all patients with pelvic malignancies undergo treatment that involves radiation. The increasing incidence is closely tied to advancements in cancer treatment. Clinicians will increasingly encounter cases of radiation proctitis due to improved overall survival in cancers such as cervical, prostate, and rectal malignancies. It is essential to understand the distinction between acute and chronic radiation proctitis, with a particular focus on chronic cases occurring more than 3 months after initial radiation treatment. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of radiation proctitis. This activity also highlights the crucial role of the interprofessional team in managing affected patients, providing clinicians with valuable insights and strategies for collaborative patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- BT target delineation
retrieved_docs.content: As part of 3-D–based planning, contouring the targets is essential. The Groupe Européen de Curiethérapie and the European Society for Radiotherapy and Oncology (GEC-ESTRO) have established standardized terminology regarding target delineation. [66] The high-risk clinical target volume (HR-CTV) consists of the entire cervix and all gross disease at the beginning of BT treatment; the intermediate-risk clinical target volume (IR-CTV) incorporates the HR-CTV and gross disease before any treatment; and the low-risk clinical target volume (LR-CTV), which includes the IR-CTV as well as the entire uterus, upper vagina, entire parametria, and spaces between the bladder and rectum. [69] Normal OAR should be contoured, including the bladder, rectum, sigmoid colon, and vagina.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Proctitis
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation proctitis can result in tenesmus and intermittent bleeding with bowel movements. Postradiation proctitis typically occurs 3 months after treatment at the earliest but may take years to develop. Treatments can include mesalamine or steroid-based suppositories to relieve pain and stop bleeding. Randomized trials have suggested that mesalamine may be slightly more efficacious than steroid-based suppositories. [71] Other treatments can include a 4% formaldehyde application to control rectal bleeding. In refractory cases, argon plasma laser coagulation may be performed on the offending vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Postoperative radiotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy with or without chemotherapy is recommended in the postoperative setting when specific pathologic findings are present. These findings are thought to represent an increased risk of recurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer Screening -- Issues of Concern -- Risk factors for CRC
retrieved_docs.content: Previously resected or diagnosed adenomatous colorectal polyps; or any of the above-mentioned genetic cancer syndromes Cystic fibrosis [11] Inflammatory bowel diseases like Crohn disease or ulcerative colitis Diabetes mellitus and insulin resistance [12] Abdomino-pelvic radiation for an earlier cancer in childhood or adolescence (as suggested by Children Oncology Group) Human immunodeficiency virus infection in men [13] Personal history Chronic alcohol use disorder Smoking Physical inactivity Obesity Poor diet (low fiber; high amounts of red and processed meat consumption) Race Higher incidence and mortality among Blacks particularly men. CRC occurrence is higher in Black individuals less than 50 years of age. Hence earlier screening, starting from the age of 45 years, is recommended by the US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- BT
retrieved_docs.content: Before undergoing BT, a review of the patient’s history, physical exam, pathology, and imaging should be conducted. A complete blood count and metabolic panel should be obtained within a week of the procedure. Metabolic derangements should be investigated and corrected before the procedure. Patients with an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) less than 500 mm should not undergo the procedure until their count has recovered. A review of medications, especially anticoagulants, should be completed, and a prothrombin time/international normalized ratio result should be obtained. Careful consideration should be given to holding anticoagulant medications before the procedure. Inpatients confined to bed should be given thromboprophylaxis and sequential compression devices. Bowel preparation should also be reviewed with the patient before the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Placement of an intracavitary applicator for an intact cervix
retrieved_docs.content: Displacement of the bladder and rectum is critical to reduce the excess dose and limit toxicity. The packing material can consist of gauze soaked in a radiopaque solution. Applicators fitted with inflatable balloons or separate rectal blades are also available. Anterior packing displaces the bladder, whereas posterior packing displaces the rectum. It is essential to ensure no packing is placed in front of the ring or ovoids, as this will significantly reduce the intended dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Clear Cell Carcinoma of the Cervix -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The sites of recurrence included the following: lung, liver, bone, brain, peritoneum, retroperitoneum, mediastinal lymph nodes, vagina and sigmoid colon. [39] Most recurrences of clear cell carcinoma of the uterine cervix are diagnosed within 3 years after primary tumor treatment. [32] However, late recurrences have been reported with a few cases, 8 years after initial diagnosis. [40] High-risk factors for recurrence include positive parametrial extension, positive pelvic lymph nodes, and positive vaginal margins. In patients with these high-risk factors, chemoradiation is the postoperative adjuvant treatment of choice. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Clinical Significance -- Cervical Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: For diagnosis, using colposcopy and biopsy is deemed safe during pregnancy. Staging is possible using a pelvic MRI without contrast. CXR and USG liver can also be utilized. PET scans are contraindicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowed therapeutic option for the management of a colonic fistula (ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ) and for the treatment of a rectal mass (قيلة مستقيمية). In both conditions, radiotherapy may be incorporated into a multidisciplinary care plan to address disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is a term used in radiotherapy to denote specific clinical conditions that are considered permissible or relevant when evaluating a patient for treatment. The allowed qualifiers for radiotherapy include respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest in adults, as well as عدوى بفيروس معوي (infection with an enteric virus). Recognizing these qualifiers helps clinicians assess patient suitability for radiotherapy, anticipate potential complications, and tailor therapeutic plans accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 662
retrieved_docs.content: “Other excision of cervix and uterus” is clinically linked to cervical stromal disorder (خلل تنسج عنق الرحم) and co‑occurs with stage 0 cervical cancer and vaginal endoscopy (منظار المهبل), highlighting its relevance to early cervical neoplasia management. This hierarchical and co‑occurrence information supports accurate coding, epidemiologic tracking, and coordinated clinical care of gynecologic surgical and oncologic interventions.

retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the bladder, cancer of the esophagus, cancer of the head and neck, cancer of other gastrointestinal organs – including the peritoneum, and cancer of other urinary organs are all associated with ورم خبيث, indicating that each of these conditions represents a malignant tumor. Recognizing that these diverse anatomical sites share the characteristic of malignancy underscores the need for oncologic evaluation, appropriate staging, and coordinated treatment strategies across these cancer types.

This co‑occurrence highlights the importance of coordinated surgical planning and comprehensive management for individuals with high‑grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2876
retrieved_docs.content: The “allowed qualifier” is a classification used to describe population‑level characteristics (epidemiology), causal factors (etiology), and therapeutic considerations (radiotherapy) for a broad spectrum of medical conditions. It applies to obstetric disorders such as preterm birth (ولادة قبل الأوان), other complications of pregnancy, and threatened abortion (تهديد بالإجهاض); infectious diseases including acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة), enteric viral infection (عدوى بفيروس معوي), and fungal infection (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ); gastrointestinal diseases such as peptic ulcer (قرحة قلاعية) and intestinal fibrosis (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ); and vascular skin lesions like varicose ulcer (تقرح الدوالي). In each of these contexts the qualifier indicates that radiotherapy may be considered despite the presence of these conditions, meaning they are not absolute contraindications. Clinicians can therefore evaluate eligibility for radiotherapy in patients with fungal infection, enteric viral infection, varicose ulceration, or a threatened miscarriage, integrating epidemiologic and etiologic information with treatment planning across obstetric, infectious, gastrointestinal, and oncologic settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1135
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the esophagus, cancer of the head and neck, cancer of other gastrointestinal organs—including the peritoneum—and cancer of other urinary organs are all associated with ورم خبيث, indicating that each of these neoplasms is a malignant tumor. Recognizing that these diverse anatomical sites share the characteristic of malignancy underscores the need for oncologic assessment and coordinated management across these organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Genitourinary System organizes several specific malignancies, including cancer of the cervix, cancer of other male genital organs, and cancer of the testis. Each of these entities is associated with the Arabic term “ورم خبيث,” indicating that they are malignant tumors. Cancer of the cervix is further classified as “سرطان عنق الرحم” and is placed under the broader category “أورام عنق الرحم الخبيثة” (malignant cervical tumors). Cancer of other male genital organs is linked to “سرطان قضيبي” (penile cancer), while cancer of the testis is linked to “سرطان الخصية” (testicular cancer) and is recognized as a disorder of the male reproductive system (“Disorder of male reproductive system” has parent “سرطان الخصية”). In addition to these genitourinary malignancies, cancers of the bladder, esophagus, head and neck, other gastrointestinal organs/peritoneum, and other urinary organs are also described as “ورم خبيث.” This hierarchical structure underscores the malignant nature of these neoplasms and provides a clear framework for their classification, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2874
retrieved_docs.content: Other excision of cervix and uterus frequently co‑occurs with سرطان عنق الرحم المرحلة 0 and with منظار المهبل. This pattern indicates that patients who undergo other excision of cervix and uterus often have early‑stage (stage 0) cervical cancer and may also receive vaginal endoscopy as part of their diagnostic or therapeutic work‑up. Recognizing these co‑occurrences helps clinicians anticipate the need for both surgical removal of cervical tissue and endoscopic evaluation of the vagina when managing early cervical neoplasia, thereby supporting more comprehensive and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2696
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain, radiotherapy, and malignant hypertension are interrelated clinical concepts. Abdominal pain is recognized as a symptom that can be clinically associated with malignant hypertension, indicating that patients with severe, uncontrolled high blood pressure may present with abdominal discomfort. In addition, abdominal pain may arise as a qualifying symptom during radiotherapy, reflecting a possible side effect or concurrent condition in patients undergoing this treatment. Radiotherapy itself is also linked to malignant hypertension, suggesting that exposure to radiation can contribute to the development or exacerbation of severe hypertension. Together, these relationships highlight the importance of monitoring abdominal pain both as a potential indicator of malignant hypertension and as a possible adverse effect of radiotherapy, while also recognizing that radiotherapy may influence blood pressure regulation in susceptible individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 651

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1674
retrieved_docs.content: Cannabis, embryology, radiotherapy and the congenital qualifier “vrozené” are each linked to a wide spectrum of medical conditions. Embryology, radiotherapy and vrozené are associated with congenital bladder fistula (ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ) and Alzheimer‑type dementia (الخرف من نوع الزهايمر). Embryology and radiotherapy also connect to malignant hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الخبيث), tissue adhesion (الالتصاق), cystitis (الْتِهابُ المثانة), mitral valve stenosis (تضيُّق الصمام المترالي) and rectal mass/rectal prolapse (قيلة مستقيمية). Abdominal pain is related to embryology, radiotherapy and vrozené and is clinically associated with malignant hypertension. The inclusion of Cannabis as a qualifier for embryology suggests a potential role of cannabis exposure in developmental or embryologic contexts. Vrozené serves as an allowed qualifier for malignant hypertension, false pseudomembranous colitis (التهاب معوي قولوني غشائي كاذب), adhesions (الالتصاق), cystitis, mitral valve stenosis and rectal prolapse, indicating that congenital variants may exist for each of these disorders. Collectively, these interrelationships illustrate how developmental biology, therapeutic interventions, congenital factors and substance exposure intersect with congenital anomalies, neurodegenerative disease, cardiovascular, urological and gastrointestinal pathologies, underscoring the need for integrated clinical assessment and multidisciplinary care.
